learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligation in India
learn.intro: India's Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) mandates DISCOMs to buy a percentage of their power from renewables. This policy drives investment in solar/wind projects, reduces fossil fuel dependence, and accelerates India's clean energy transition. For DISCOMs, compliance means balancing costs with grid stability while meeting national climate goals.
learn.c1_text: RPO requires DISCOMs to source a fixed percentage of electricity from renewables. For example, India targets 43% by 2022. This forces utilities to invest in solar/wind farms, creating jobs and lowering long-term electricity prices through competition.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs face high upfront costs for renewable projects and grid integration issues. However, subsidies and auctions (like India's record-low solar tariffs) make compliance feasible. Failure risks penalties or loss of consumer trust.
learn.c3_text: India's RPO has spurred 100+ GW of renewable capacity. States like Gujarat lead in solar adoption. Policies like RPO 2.0 aim to increase targets to 50% by 2030, aligning with India's net-zero pledge by 2070.
learn.stat3: Solar tariffs dropped 80% since 2010 due to RPO
learn.stat4: DISCOMs face ₹50,000 crores in annual RPO compliance costs

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels Explained
learn.intro: Bifacial solar panels generate power from both sides, boosting efficiency. They capture sunlight reflected from the ground or water, making them ideal for large-scale solar farms. This technology is key to maximizing limited land use in India's solar push.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels have transparent backsheets. They absorb direct sunlight on the front and reflected light on the back. For example, a 20% gain in output is possible compared to monofacial panels, depending on ground reflectivity.
learn.c2_text: In desert regions with high albedo (reflectivity), bifacial panels can produce 15-20% more energy. A 1 MW bifacial system in Rajasthan might generate 1.2 MW annually vs. 1 MW for standard panels.
learn.c3_text: India's largest solar parks now use bifacial panels. Projects like Bhadla Solar Park leverage their efficiency to meet RPO targets faster. Costs have dropped 30% since 2020, making them competitive with monofacial panels.
learn.stat1: Bifacial panels can boost output by 15-25%
learn.stat2: Rajasthan's Bhadla Solar Park uses 100% bifacial panels
learn.stat3: Bifacial module costs now rival monofacial at ₹15/watt
learn.stat4: India aims for 10 GW bifacial installations by 2025

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture
learn.intro: Direct Air Carbon Capture (DAC) removes CO2 from the atmosphere using renewable energy. This technology is critical for achieving net-zero emissions, as it addresses historical emissions not covered by other methods. Pairing DAC with renewables ensures it's carbon-neutral.
learn.c1_text: DAC uses chemical processes to absorb CO2 from air. Renewables power the energy-intensive process. For example, a plant might capture 1 ton of CO2 daily, requiring 1 MWh of renewable electricity per ton.
learn.c2_text: Current DAC plants are small and costly (~$600/ton CO2). Scaling requires cheaper renewables and government incentives. Projects like Climeworks in Switzerland show feasibility but need policy support to expand.
learn.c3_text: India's DAC potential lies in industrial clusters. The National Clean Energy and Environment Mission supports pilot projects. Using solar/wind power for DAC aligns with India's 2030 net-zero targets.

learn.title: Sodium-Ion Batteries
learn.intro: Sodium-ion batteries offer a cheaper, safer alternative to lithium-ion. They use abundant sodium, reducing reliance on scarce lithium. India's push for affordable energy storage makes this technology vital for grid stability and EV adoption.
learn.c1_text: Sodium is 100x more abundant than lithium. Batteries use sodium ions instead of lithium, lowering material costs. For example, a sodium-ion battery might cost ₹10,000/kWh vs. ₹15,000/kWh for lithium-ion.
learn.c2_text: Sodium-ion batteries have lower energy density (120-160 Wh/kg vs. 250+ for lithium-ion). However, they excel in cycle life (3,000+ cycles) and safety, making them ideal for stationary storage in India's solar farms.
learn.c3_text: Indian startups like Indian Energy Storage Solutions (IES) are commercializing sodium-ion batteries. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme targets 10 GWh production by 2025, reducing import dependency.
learn.stat3: India's PLI target: 10 GWh sodium-ion storage by 2025
learn.stat4: Sodium abundance: 2.5x lithium in Earth's crust

learn.title: Virtual Power Plants
learn.intro: Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) use AI to manage millions of distributed energy sources like rooftop solar and EVs. This technology optimizes grid stability and reduces reliance on fossil fuels. India's decentralized energy systems make VPPs a game-changer for urban and rural electrification.
learn.c1_text: A VPP aggregates small energy sources (solar panels, batteries) into a single grid asset. AI algorithms balance supply and demand in real-time. For example, excess solar power can charge EVs or feed back to the grid during peaks.
learn.c2_text: Machine learning predicts energy usage patterns. In a VPP, AI might delay EV charging during high demand or divert solar power to storage. A 1 MW VPP can serve 1,000 households, reducing grid stress.
learn.c3_text: India's 300 GW renewable target by 2030 creates VPP opportunities. Projects like Kerala's VPP integrate 500+ solar rooftops. AI-driven VPPs can cut distribution losses by 15% in urban areas.
learn.stat1: VPPs can reduce grid costs by 10-15%

learn.title: Solar Inverters Explained
learn.intro: Solar inverters convert DC solar power to AC for home use. Choosing between string, micro, or hybrid inverters affects efficiency and cost. Understanding these differences helps homeowners and installers optimize solar systems in India's diverse climate.
learn.c1_text: String inverters handle multiple panels in series. They're cost-effective but if one panel underperforms (e.g., shaded), the whole string loses output. Ideal for uniform installations with minimal shading.
learn.c2_text: Micro inverters attach to each panel, optimizing individual performance. If one panel is shaded, others still produce power. They cost 20-30% more but increase overall efficiency by 5-10% in partial shade.
learn.c3_text: Hybrid inverters manage solar and battery storage. They allow excess solar to charge batteries or feed the grid. In India, they support off-grid homes and EV charging, with costs now competitive at ₹20/watt.
learn.stat2: Micro inverters: 5-10% efficiency gain in shade
learn.stat3: Hybrid inverters: Enable solar + storage systems
